subject: Chemistry
question_text: The decomposition of N 2 O 5 in CCl 4 at 318K has been studied by monitoring the concentration of N 2 O 5 in the solution. Initially the concentration of N 2 O 5 is 2.33 mol L -1 and after 184 minutes, it is reduced to 2.08 mol L -1 . The reaction takes place according to the equation. 2 N 2 O 5 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4NO 2 (g) + O 2 (g). Calculate the average rate of this reaction in terms of hours, minutes, and seconds. What is the rate of production of NO 2 during this period?
question_html:
The decomposition of N2O5 in CCl4 at 318K has been studied by monitoring the concentration of N2O5 in the solution. Initially the concentration of N2O5 is 2.33 mol L-1 and after 184 minutes, it is reduced to 2.08 mol L-1. The reaction takes place according to the equation.
2 N2O5 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4NO2 (g) + O2 (g).
Calculate the average rate of this reaction in terms of hours, minutes, and seconds. What is the rate of production of NO2 during this period?
solution_text: Average Rate of reaction {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left\{-\frac{\triangle\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\triangle t}\right\}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{(2.08-2.33) \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{184 \mathrm{min}}\right]{/tex} = 6.79 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 /min = (6.79 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L -1 min -1 ) {tex}\times{/tex} (60 min/1h) The average rate of this reaction in terms of hours = 4.07 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 mol L -1 / The average rate of this reaction in terms of minutes = 6.79 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1min/60s The average rate of this reaction in terms of seconds = 1.13 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 mol L -1 s -1 It may be remembered that Rate {tex}=\frac{1}{4}\left\{\frac{\triangle\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\triangle t}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\triangle\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\triangle t}{/tex} = 6.79 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 mol L -1 min -1 = 2.72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 mol L -1 min -1
solution_html:
Average Rate of reaction {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left\{-\frac{\triangle\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\triangle t}\right\}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{(2.08-2.33) \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{184 \mathrm{min}}\right]{/tex}
= 6.79 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1/min = (6.79 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1 min-1) {tex}\times{/tex} (60 min/1h)
The average rate of this reaction in terms of hours = 4.07 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 mol L-1/
The average rate of this reaction in terms of minutes = 6.79 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1min/60s
The average rate of this reaction in terms of seconds = 1.13 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 mol L-1 s-1
It may be remembered that
Rate {tex}=\frac{1}{4}\left\{\frac{\triangle\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\triangle t}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\triangle\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\triangle t}{/tex} = 6.79 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 mol L-1 min-1 = 2.72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mol L-1 min-1

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Calculate the overall order of a reaction which has the rate expression. Rate = k [A] {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} [B] {tex}\frac {3} {2}{/tex} Rate = k [A] {tex}\frac {3} {2}{/tex} [B] -1
question_html:
Calculate the overall order of a reaction which has the rate expression.

  1. Rate = k [A]{tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} [B]{tex}\frac {3} {2}{/tex}
  2. Rate = k [A]{tex}\frac {3} {2}{/tex} [B]-1
solution_text: Rate = k [A] x [B] y order of a reaction = x + y So order = {tex}\frac {1} {2} + \frac {3} {2}{/tex} = 2, i.e., second order order = {tex}\frac {3} {2}{/tex} + (-1) = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex} , i.e., half order.
solution_html:
1. Rate = k [A]x [B]y
    order of a reaction = x + y
    So order = {tex}\frac {1} {2} + \frac {3} {2}{/tex} = 2, i.e.,  second order
  2. order = {tex}\frac {3} {2}{/tex} + (-1) = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex}, i.e., half order.

question_html:

  2. k = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 s-1
solution_text: Since the units of rate constant are L mol -1 s -1 , the reaction is of second order. Since the units of rate constant are s -1 , the reaction is of first order.

1. Since the units of rate constant are L mol-1 s-1, the reaction is of second order.
  2. Since the units of rate constant are s-1, the reaction is of first order.

question_text: The initial concentration of N 2 O 5 in the first order reaction N 2 O 5 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NO 2 + ½O 2 was 1.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 mol L -1 at 318 K. The concentration of N 2 O 5 after 60 minutes was 0.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 mol L -1 . Calculate the rate constant of the reaction at 318 K.
question_html: The initial concentration of N2O5 in the first order reaction N2O5 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NO2 + ½O2 was 1.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 mol L-1 at 318 K. The concentration of N2O5 after 60 minutes was 0.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 mol L-1. Calculate the rate constant of the reaction at 318 K.
solution_text: {tex}\mathrm{K}=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{A}]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]_{0}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]_{t}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{60} \log \frac{1.24 \times 10^{-2}}{0.2 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{60} \log 6.2{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{60} \times 0.7924 \mathrm{min}^{-1}{/tex} = 0.0304 min -1
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{K}=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{A}]}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]_{0}}{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]_{t}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2.303}{60} \log \frac{1.24 \times 10^{-2}}{0.2 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2.303}{60} \log 6.2{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2.303}{60} \times 0.7924 \mathrm{min}^{-1}{/tex}
= 0.0304 min-1

question_text: The following data were obtained during the first order thermal decomposition of N 2 O 5 (g) at constant volume: 2N 2 O 5 (g) {tex}\to{/tex} 2N 2 O 4 (g) + O 2 (g) S. No. {tex}\frac {Time} {s}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{\text{Total}}\,{\text{Pressure}}\,}}{{{\text{(atm)}}}}{/tex} a. 0 0.5 b. 100 0.512 Calculate the rate constant.
question_html:
The following data were obtained during the first order thermal decomposition of N2O5 (g) at constant volume:
2N2O5 (g) {tex}\to{/tex} 2N2O4 (g) + O2 (g)

S. No. {tex}\frac {Time} {s}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{\text{Total}}\,{\text{Pressure}}\,}}{{{\text{(atm)}}}}{/tex}
a.  0 0.5
b. 100 0.512

Calculate the rate constant.
solution_text: Let the pressure of N 2 O 5 (g) decrease by 2x atm. As two moles of N 2 O 5 decomposes to give two moles of N 2 O 4 (g) and one mole of O 2 (g), the pressure of N 2 O 4 (g) increases by 2x atm and that of O 2 (g) increases by x atm. 2N 2 O 5 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2N 2 O 4 (g) + O 2 (g) Start t = 0 0.5 atm 0 atm 0 atm At time t (0.5 - 2x) atm 2x atm x atm {tex}{{\text{P}}_{\text{t}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}{/tex} = (0.5 - 2x) + 2x + x = 0.5 + x x = p t - 0.5 {tex}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{/tex} = 0.5 - 2x = 0.5 - 2(p t - 0.5) = 1.5 - 2p t At t = 100 s; p t = 0.512 atm {tex}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{/tex} = 1.5 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 = 0.476 atm. Rate constant k = 4.98 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 s -1
solution_html:
Let the pressure of N2O5 (g) decrease by 2x atm. As two moles of N2O5 decomposes to give two moles of N2O4 (g) and one mole of O2 (g), the pressure of N2O4 (g) increases by 2x atm and that of O2 (g) increases by x atm.

  2N2O5 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2N2O4 (g)  +  O2 (g)
Start t = 0 0.5 atm   0 atm   0 atm
At time t (0.5 - 2x) atm   2x atm   x atm

{tex}{{\text{P}}_{\text{t}}}{\text{ = }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}{/tex}
= (0.5 - 2x) + 2x + x = 0.5 + x
x = pt - 0.5
{tex}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{/tex} = 0.5 - 2x
= 0.5 - 2(pt - 0.5) = 1.5 - 2pt
At t = 100 s; pt = 0.512 atm
{tex}{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{5}}}}}{/tex} = 1.5 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 = 0.476 atm.
Rate constant k = 4.98{tex}\times{/tex}10-4s-1

subject: Chemistry
question_text: A first order reaction is found to have a rate constant k = 5.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -14 s -1 . Find the half-life of reaction.
question_html: A first order reaction is found to have a rate constant k = 5.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-14 s-1. Find the half-life of reaction.
solution_html:
For a first order reaction, {tex}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{{0.693}}{k}{/tex}

So, {tex}{t_{1/2}}= \frac{{0.693}}{{5.5 \times {{10}^{ - 14}}{\rm{ se}}{{\rm{c}}^{{\rm{ - 1}}}}{\rm{ }}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.26}} \times {\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{13}}}}{\rm{ s}}{/tex}

question_html: Show that in a first order reaction, time required for completion of 99.9% is 10 times of half-life (t1/2) of the reaction.
solution_text: When reaction is completed 99.9%, [R] n = [R] 0 - 0.999[R] 0 rate constant is {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{R}]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}-0.999[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{t} \log 10^{3}{/tex} t = 6.909 / k For half-life of the reaction t 1/2 = 0.693 / k {tex}\frac{t}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{6.909}{k} {/tex} {tex}\times \frac{k}{0.693}=10{/tex}
solution_html:
When reaction is completed 99.9%, [R]n = [R]0 - 0.999[R]0 rate constant is
{tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{R}]}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}-0.999[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{t} \log 10^{3}{/tex}
t = 6.909 / k
For half-life of the reaction
t1/2 = 0.693 / k

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The rate constants of a reaction at 500K and 700K are 0.02s -1 and 0.07s -1 respectively. Calculate the values of E a and A.
question_html: The rate constants of a reaction at 500K and 700K are 0.02s-1 and 0.07s-1 respectively. Calculate the values of Ea and A.
solution_text: {tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\log \frac{0.07}{0.02}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{E_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \times 8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}\right){/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{700-500}{700 \times 500}\right]{/tex} 0.544 = E a {tex}\times{/tex} 5.714 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{19.15}}{/tex} Activation energy E a = 0.544 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{{19.15}}{{5.714}} \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} = 18230.8 J Since rate constant K = Ae {tex}\frac{-E_a}{RT}{/tex} 0.02 = {tex}{\text{A}}{{\text{e}}^{\frac{{{\text{ - 18230}}{\text{.8}}}}{{{\text{8}}{\text{.314}}}} \times 500}}{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{{0.02}}{{0.012}}{/tex} = 1.61
solution_html:
{tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\log \frac{0.07}{0.02}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{E_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \times 8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}\right){/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{700-500}{700 \times 500}\right]{/tex}
0.544 = Ea {tex}\times{/tex} 5.714 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{19.15}}{/tex}
Activation energy Ea = 0.544 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{{19.15}}{{5.714}} \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} = 18230.8 J
Since rate constant  K = Ae{tex}\frac{-E_a}{RT}{/tex}
0.02 = {tex}{\text{A}}{{\text{e}}^{\frac{{{\text{ - 18230}}{\text{.8}}}}{{{\text{8}}{\text{.314}}}} \times 500}}{/tex}
A = {tex}\frac{{0.02}}{{0.012}}{/tex} = 1.61

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The first order rate constant for the decomposition of ethyl iodide by the reaction C 2 H 5 I(g) {tex}\to{/tex} C 2 H 4 (g) + HI(g) at 600 K is 1.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 s -1 . Its energy of activation is 209 kJ/mol. Calculate the rate constant of the reaction at 700 K.
question_html:
The first order rate constant for the decomposition of ethyl iodide by the reaction
C2H5I(g) {tex}\to{/tex} C2H4(g) + HI(g)
at 600 K is 1.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 s-1. Its energy of activation is 209 kJ/mol. Calculate the rate constant of the reaction at 700 K.
solution_html:
Here {tex}{T_1} = 600K{/tex} 
{tex}{T_2} = 700K{/tex}
{tex}{E_a} = 209KJ/mol{/tex}
{tex} = 209000Jmo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}{k_1} = 1.60 \times {10^{ - 5}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}{k_2} = ?{/tex}
Using the formula
{tex}\log \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_1}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303R}}\left[ {\frac{{{T_2} - {T_1}}}{{{T_1}{T_2}}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\log {k_2} - \log {k_1} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303R}}\left[ {\frac{{700 - 600}}{{600 \times 700}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\log {k_2} - \log 1.60 \times {10^{ - 5}} = \frac{{209000}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}{/tex}{tex}\left[ {\frac{{100}}{{600 \times 700}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\log {k_2} = \log 1.60 \times {10^{ - 5}} + 2.599{/tex}
{tex}\log {k_2} = - 4.796 + 2.599{/tex}
= - 2.197
{tex}{k_2}{/tex}= anti log (-2.197)
{tex} = 6.36 \times {10^{ - 3}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: For the reaction R {tex}\to{/tex} P, the concentration of a reactant changes from 0.03 M to 0.02 M in 25 minutes. Calculate the average rate of reaction using units of time both in minutes and seconds.
question_html: For the reaction R {tex}\to{/tex} P, the concentration of a reactant changes from 0.03 M to 0.02 M in 25 minutes. Calculate the average rate of reaction using units of time both in minutes and seconds.
solution_html:
Average rate of reaction {tex} - \frac{{\Delta \left[ R \right]}}{{\Delta t}}{/tex}
{tex} = - \frac{{{{\left[ R \right]}_2} - {{\left[ R \right]}_1}}}{{{t_2} - {t_1}}} = - \frac{{0.02 - 0.03}}{{25}}M\,{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex} = - \frac{{ - 0.01}}{{25}}M\,{\min ^{ - 1}} = 4 \times {10^{ - 4}}M\,{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{60}}\,M\,{s^{ - 1}} = 6.67 \times {10^{ - 6}}M{\rm{ }}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}

question_text: In a reaction 2A {tex}\to{/tex} Products, the concentration of A decreases from 0.5 mol L -1 to 0.4 mol L -1 in 10 minutes. Calculate the rate during this interval.
question_html: In a reaction 2A {tex}\to{/tex} Products, the concentration of A decreases from 0.5 mol L-1 to 0.4 mol L-1 in 10 minutes. Calculate the rate during this interval.
solution_text: Average rate {tex} = - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{\Delta [A]}}{{\Delta t}}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{[{A_2}] - {{[A]}_1}}}{{{t_2} - {t_1}}}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{1}{2}\times\frac{{0.4M - 0.5M}}{{10\min }}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{1}{2}\times\frac{{ - 0.1M}}{{10\,\min }}{/tex} The rate of reaction is {tex} = 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}M\,{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Average rate {tex} = - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{\Delta [A]}}{{\Delta t}}{/tex}
{tex} = - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{[{A_2}] - {{[A]}_1}}}{{{t_2} - {t_1}}}{/tex}
{tex} = - \frac{1}{2}\times\frac{{0.4M - 0.5M}}{{10\min }}{/tex}
{tex} = - \frac{1}{2}\times\frac{{ - 0.1M}}{{10\,\min }}{/tex}
The rate of reaction is {tex} = 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}M\,{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The conversion of molecules X to Y follows second order kinetics. If concentration of X is increased to three times how will it affect the rate of formation of Y?
question_html: The conversion of molecules X to Y follows second order kinetics. If concentration of X is increased to three times how will it affect the rate of formation of Y?
solution_text: The reaction {tex} X \to Y{/tex} follows second order kinetics. Therefore, the rate equation for this reaction will be: Rate = {tex}k{\left[ X \right]^2}{/tex} (1) Let [X] = a {tex}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} , then equation (1) can be written as: Rate 1 = k(a) 2 = ka 2 If the concentration of X is increased to three times, then [X] = 3 a mol L -1 Now, the rate equation will be: Rate = k(3a) 2 = 9(ka 2 ) Hence, the rate of formation will increase by 9 times.
solution_html:
The reaction {tex} X \to Y{/tex} follows second order kinetics.
Therefore, the rate equation for this reaction will be:
Rate = {tex}k{\left[ X \right]^2}{/tex} (1)
Let [X] = a {tex}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}, then equation (1) can be written as:
Rate1 = k(a)2 = ka2
If the concentration of X is increased to three times, then [X] = 3 a mol L-1
Now, the rate equation will be:
Rate = k(3a)2 = 9(ka2)
Hence, the rate of formation will increase by 9 times.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: A first order reaction has a rate constant 1.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 s -1 . How long will 5 g of this reactant take to reduce to 3 g?
question_html: A first order reaction has a rate constant 1.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 s-1. How long will 5 g of this reactant take to reduce to 3 g?
solution_text: Initial amount = 5 g Final amount = 3 g Rate constant = 1.15 × 10 -3 s -1 For a 1st order reaction: {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{{{\left[ R \right]}_0}}}{{\left[ R \right]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.15 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}\log \frac{5}{3} = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.15 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times 0.2219{/tex} = 444.38 s or 444 s (approx).

{tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.15 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}\log \frac{5}{3} = \frac{{2.303}}{{1.15 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times 0.2219{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What will be the effect of temperature on rate constant?
question_html: What will be the effect of temperature on rate constant?
solution_text: Rate constant of a reaction is nearly doubled with rise in temperature by 10°. The exact dependence of the rate constant on temperature is given by Arrhenius equation, k = A e -E a /RT , where A is the Arrhenius factor or the frequency factor. It is also called pre-exponential factor. It is a constant specific to a particular reaction. R is gas constant and E a is activation energy measured in joules/mole (J mol -1 ).

question_text: The rate of the chemical reaction doubles for an increase of 10 K in absolute temperature from 298 K. Calculate {tex} {E_a}.{/tex}
question_html: The rate of the chemical reaction doubles for an increase of 10 K in absolute temperature from 298 K. Calculate{tex} {E_a}.{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that {tex}T_1{/tex} = 298 K Therefore, {tex} {T_2} = (298 + 10)K{/tex} = 308 K We also know that the rate of the reaction doubles when temperature is increased by {tex}{10^0}.{/tex} Therefore, let us take the value of {tex}{k_1} = k{/tex} and that of {tex}{k_2} = 2k{/tex} Also, {tex}R = 8.314\,J\,{K^{ - 1}}\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Now, substituting these values in the equation: {tex}\log \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_1}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,R}}\left[ {\frac{{{T_2} - {T_1}}}{{{T_1}{T_2}}}} \right]{/tex} We get: {tex}\log \frac{{2k}}{k} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left[ {\frac{{10}}{{298 \times 308}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\log 2 = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left[ {\frac{{10}}{{298 \times 308}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}{E_a} = \frac{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times 308 \times \log 2}}{{10}}{/tex} {tex} = 52897.78\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}} = 52.9\,k\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}T_1{/tex} = 298 K
Therefore, {tex} {T_2} = (298 + 10)K{/tex}= 308 K
We also know that the rate of the reaction doubles when temperature is increased by {tex}{10^0}.{/tex}

Therefore, let us take the value of {tex}{k_1} = k{/tex} and that of {tex}{k_2} = 2k{/tex}
Also, {tex}R = 8.314\,J\,{K^{ - 1}}\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Now, substituting these values in the equation:
{tex}\log \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_1}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,R}}\left[ {\frac{{{T_2} - {T_1}}}{{{T_1}{T_2}}}} \right]{/tex}
We get:
{tex}\log \frac{{2k}}{k} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left[ {\frac{{10}}{{298 \times 308}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\log 2 = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left[ {\frac{{10}}{{298 \times 308}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}{E_a} = \frac{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times 308 \times \log 2}}{{10}}{/tex}
{tex} = 52897.78\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}} = 52.9\,k\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}

question_text: The activation energy for the reaction 2 HI(g) {tex}\to{/tex} H 2 + I 2 (g) is 209.5 kJ mol -1 at 581 K. Calculate the fraction of molecules of reactants having energy equal to or greater than activation energy?
question_html:
The activation energy for the reaction
2 HI(g) {tex}\to{/tex} H2 + I2(g)
is 209.5 kJ mol-1 at 581 K. Calculate the fraction of molecules of reactants having energy equal to or greater than activation energy?
solution_text: Fraction of molecules having energy equal to or greater than activation energy. {tex} = n = \frac{n}{W} = {e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \log \,n\, = - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{RT}}{/tex} .. or log n {tex} = - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex} . {tex} = - \frac{{209.5 \times {{10}^3}J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314\,J{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 581\,K}}{/tex} . {tex}= - 18.8323{/tex} . n = Anti log (-18.8323). = Antilog {tex}\overline {19} .1677{/tex} . {tex} = 1.471 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Fraction of molecules having energy equal to or greater than activation energy.
{tex} = n = \frac{n}{W} = {e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore \log \,n\, = - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{RT}}{/tex}..
or log n {tex} = - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex}.
{tex} = - \frac{{209.5 \times {{10}^3}J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314\,J{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 581\,K}}{/tex}.
{tex}= - 18.8323{/tex}.
n = Anti log (-18.8323).
= Antilog {tex}\overline {19} .1677{/tex}.
{tex} = 1.471 \times {10^{ - 19}}{/tex}.

question_text: From the rate expression for the following reactions, determine their order of reaction and the dimensions of the rate constants. 3NO {tex}\to{/tex} N 2 O(g) Rate = k[NO] 2 H 2 O 2 (aq) + 3I - (aq) + 2H + {tex}\to{/tex} 2H 2 O(I) + {tex}\mathrm{I}_3^{-}{/tex} Rate = k[H 2 O 2 ] [I - ] CH 3 CHO (g) {tex}\to{/tex} CH 4 (g) + CO(g) Rate = k [CH 3 CHO] 3/2 C 2 H 5 Cl (g) {tex}\to{/tex} C 2 H 4 (g) + HCl (g) Rate = k [C 2 H 5 Cl]

From the rate expression for the following reactions, determine their order of reaction and the dimensions of the rate constants.

  1. 3NO {tex}\to{/tex} N2O(g) Rate = k[NO]2

solution_text: Given rate = {tex} k{\left[ {NO} \right]^2}{/tex} Therefore, order of the reaction = 2 Dimension of {tex}k = \frac{{Rate}}{{{{\left[ {NO} \right]}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{mol\,{L^{ - 1}}\,{s^{ - 1}}}}{{{{(mol\,{L^{ - 1}})}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}}}{{mo{l^2}{L^{ - 2}}}} = L\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} the dimension of rate constants is L mol -1 s -1 Given rate = {tex}k\left[ {{H_2}{O_2}} \right]\left[ {{I^ - }} \right]{/tex} Therefore, the order of the reaction = 2 Dimension of {tex} k = \frac{{Rate}}{{\left[ {{H_2}{O_2}} \right]\left[ {{I^ - }} \right]}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}}}{{(mol\,{L^{ - 1}})(mol\,{L^{ - 1}})}}{/tex} {tex} = L\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Given rate = {tex} k{\left[ {C{H_3}CHO} \right]^{3/2}}{/tex} Therefore, the order of reaction = {tex} \frac{3}{2}{/tex} Dimension of {tex}k = \frac{{Rate}}{{{{\left[ {C{H_3}CHO} \right]}^{\frac{3}{2}}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{mol\,{L^{ - 1}}\,{s^{ - 1}}}}{{{{(mol\,{L^{ - 1}})}^{\frac{3}{2}}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}}}{{mo{l^{^{\frac{3}{2}}}}{L^{ - \frac{3}{2}}}}} = {L^{\frac{1}{2}}}\,mo{l^{ - \frac{1}{2}}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Given rate = {tex}k\left[ {{C_2}{H_5}Cl} \right]{/tex} Therefore, the order of the reaction = 1 Dimension of {tex}k = \frac{{Rate}}{{\left[ {{C_2}{H_5}Cl} \right]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}}}{{mol\,{L^{ - 1}}}} = {s^{ - 1}}{/tex}

1. Given rate ={tex} k{\left[ {NO} \right]^2}{/tex}
    Therefore, order of the reaction = 2

subject: Chemistry
question_text: For the reaction: 2A + B {tex}\to{/tex} A 2 B the rate = k[A][B] 2 with k = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 mol -2 L 2 s -1 . Calculate the initial rate of the reaction when [A] = 0.1 mol L -1 , [B] = 0.2 mol L -1 . Calculate the rate of reaction after [A] is reduced to 0.06 mol L -1 .
question_html:
For the reaction:
2A + B {tex}\to{/tex} A2B
the rate = k[A][B]2 with k = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 mol-2 L2 s-1. Calculate the initial rate of the reaction when [A] = 0.1 mol L-1, [B] = 0.2 mol L-1. Calculate the rate of reaction after [A] is reduced to 0.06 mol L-1.
solution_text: The initial rate of the reaction is: Rate = {tex} k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} {tex} = \left( {2.0 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}mo{l^{ - 2}}{L^2}{s^{ - 1}}} \right)\left( {0.1mol{\mkern 1mu} {L^{ - 1}}} \right){/tex} {tex}{\left( {0.2mol{\mkern 1mu} {L^{ - 1}}} \right)^2} = 8.0 \times {10^{ - 9}}mo{l^{ - 2}}{L^2}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} When [A] is reduced from 0.1mol L -1 to 0.06 mol -1 , the concentration of A reacted = {tex} \left( {0.1 - 0.06} \right)mol\,{L^{ - 1}} = 0.004\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} Therefore, concentration of B reacted {tex} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.04\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}} = 0.02mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} Then, concentration of B available, {tex}[B] = (0.2 - 0.02)mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} = 0.18 mol L -1 After [A] is reduced to 0.06 mol -1 , the rate of the reaction is given by, Rate = {tex} k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} {tex} = \left( {2.0 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}mo{l^{ - 2}}{L^2}{s^{ - 1}}} \right)\left( {0.06mol{\mkern 1mu} {L^{ - 1}}} \right){/tex} (0.18 ) 2 = 3.89 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 mol -1 L -1 s -1
solution_html:
The initial rate of the reaction is:
Rate = {tex} k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex}
{tex} = \left( {2.0 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}mo{l^{ - 2}}{L^2}{s^{ - 1}}} \right)\left( {0.1mol{\mkern 1mu} {L^{ - 1}}} \right){/tex} {tex}{\left( {0.2mol{\mkern 1mu} {L^{ - 1}}} \right)^2} = 8.0 \times {10^{ - 9}}mo{l^{ - 2}}{L^2}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
When [A] is reduced from  0.1mol L-1 to 0.06 mol-1, the concentration of A reacted
= {tex} \left( {0.1 - 0.06} \right)mol\,{L^{ - 1}} = 0.004\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Therefore, concentration of B reacted {tex} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.04\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}} = 0.02mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Then, concentration of B available, {tex}[B] = (0.2 - 0.02)mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
 = 0.18 mol L-1
After [A] is reduced to  0.06 mol-1, the rate of the reaction is given by,
Rate = {tex} k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex}
{tex} = \left( {2.0 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}mo{l^{ - 2}}{L^2}{s^{ - 1}}} \right)\left( {0.06mol{\mkern 1mu} {L^{ - 1}}} \right){/tex} (0.18 )2
= 3.89 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 mol-1 L-1s-1

question_text: The decomposition of NH 3 on platinum surface is zero order reaction. What are the rates of production of N 2 and H 2 if k = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 ?
question_html: The decomposition of NH3 on platinum surface is zero order reaction. What are the rates of production of N2 and H2 if k = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1 s-1?
solution_text: {tex}2N{H_3} \to {N_2} + 3{H_2}{/tex} Rate {tex} = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = +\frac{{ d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = + \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} For zero order reaction rate = k {tex}\therefore - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Rate of production of {tex}{N_2} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Rate of production of {tex}{H_2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = k{/tex} {tex}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = 3k{/tex} {tex} = 3 \times 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex} = 7.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}2N{H_3} \to {N_2} + 3{H_2}{/tex}
Rate {tex} = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = +\frac{{ d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = + \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex}
For zero order reaction rate = k
{tex}\therefore - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Rate of production of {tex}{N_2} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Rate of production of {tex}{H_2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = k{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = 3k{/tex}
{tex} = 3 \times 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex} = 7.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: A reaction is second order with respect to a reactant. How is the rate of reaction affected if the concentration of the reactant is: doubled reduced to half
question_html:
A reaction is second order with respect to a reactant. How is the rate of reaction affected if the concentration of the reactant is:

  1. doubled
  2. reduced to half
solution_text: Let the concentration of the reactant be [A] = a Rate of reaction, {tex} R = k{\left[ A \right]^2}{/tex} {tex} = k{a^2}{/tex} If the concentration of the reactant is doubled, i.e. [A] = 2a, then the rate of the reaction would be {tex}R' = k{(2a)^2} = 4k{a^2}{/tex} = 4R Therefore, the rate of the reaction would increase by 4 times. If the concentration of the reactant is reduced to half, i.e., {tex}[A] = \frac{1}{2}a{/tex} then the rate of the reaction would be {tex} R' = k{\left( {\frac{1}{2}a} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{4}k{a^2} = \frac{1}{4}R {/tex} Therefore, the rate of the reaction would be reduced to {tex}\frac{1}{4}th{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let the concentration of the reactant be [A] = a
Rate of reaction,{tex} R = k{\left[ A \right]^2}{/tex}{tex} = k{a^2}{/tex}

  1. If the concentration of the reactant is doubled, i.e. [A] = 2a, then the rate of the reaction would be
    {tex}R' = k{(2a)^2} = 4k{a^2}{/tex}
    = 4R
    Therefore, the rate of the reaction would increase by 4 times.
  2. If the concentration of the reactant is reduced to half, i.e.,{tex}[A] = \frac{1}{2}a{/tex} then the rate of the reaction would be {tex} R' = k{\left( {\frac{1}{2}a} \right)^2}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{1}{4}k{a^2} = \frac{1}{4}R {/tex} 
    Therefore, the rate of the reaction would be reduced to {tex}\frac{1}{4}th{/tex}.

question_text: What is the effect of temperature on the rate constant of a reaction? How can this effect of temperature on rate constant be represented quantitatively?
question_html: What is the effect of temperature on the rate constant of a reaction? How can this effect of temperature on rate constant be represented quantitatively?
solution_text: The rate constant is nearly doubled with a rise in temperature by 10 0 C for a chemical reaction.The temperature effect on the rate constant can be represented quantitatively by Arrhenius equation, {tex}k = A{e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex} where, k is the rate constant, A is the Arrhenius factor or the frequency factor, R is the gas constant, T is the temperature, and is the energy of activation for the reaction.
solution_html:
The rate constant is nearly doubled with a rise in temperature by 10 0C for a chemical reaction.The temperature effect on the rate constant can be represented quantitatively by Arrhenius equation, 
{tex}k = A{e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex}where, k is the rate constant, A is the Arrhenius factor or the frequency factor, R is the gas constant, T is the temperature, and  is the energy of activation for the reaction.

solution_text: Average rate of reaction during the interval 30 - 60 seconds. {tex}=\frac{C_{2}-C_{1}}{t_{2}-t_{1}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.31-0.17}{60-30}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.14}{30}{/tex} = 4.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 mol L -1 s -1

subject: Chemistry
question_text: A reaction is first order in A and second order in B. Write the differential rate equation. How is the rate affected on increasing the concentration of B three times? How is the rate affected when the concentrations of both A and B are doubled?
question_html:
A reaction is first order in A and second order in B.

  1. Write the differential rate equation.
  2. How is the rate affected on increasing the concentration of B three times?
  3. How is the rate affected when the concentrations of both A and B are doubled?
solution_text: It is given that a reaction is first order in A and second order in B. The differential rate equation will be {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} If the concentration of B is increased three times, then {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[3B]^2} = 9.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 9 times. When the concentrations of both A and B are doubled, {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2} = k[2A]{[2B]^2} = 8.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 8 times.
solution_html:
It is given that a reaction is first order in A and second order in B.

  1. The differential rate equation will be
    {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex}
  2. If the concentration of B is increased three times, then
    {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[3B]^2} = 9.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex}
    Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 9 times.
  3. When the concentrations of both A and B are doubled,
    {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2} = k[2A]{[2B]^2} = 8.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex}
    Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 8 times.

Dividing equation (i) by (ii), we obtain
{tex}\frac{5.07\times {{10}^{-5}}}{5.07\times {{10}^{-5}}}=\frac{k{{[0.20]}^{x}}{{[0.30]}^{y}}}{k{{[0.20]}^{x}}{{[0.10]}^{y}}}{/tex}
{tex}1=\frac{{{[0.30]}^{y}}}{{{[0.10]}^{y}}}{/tex}
{tex}{{\left( \frac{0.30}{0.10} \right)}^{0}}={{\left( \frac{0.30}{0.10} \right)}^{y}}{/tex}
y = 0
Dividing equation (iii) by (ii), we obtain
{tex} \frac{1.43\times {{10}^{-4}}}{5.07\times {{10}^{-5}}}=\frac{k{{[0.40]}^{x}}{{[0.05]}^{y}}}{k{{[0.20]}^{x}}{{[0.30]}^{y}}}{/tex}

Since y=0,
{tex} {{[0.05]}^{y}}={{[0.30]}^{y}}=1 {/tex}

{tex}\frac{1.43\times {{10}^{-4}}}{5.07\times {{10}^{-5}}}=\frac{{{[0.40]}^{x}}}{{{[0.20]}^{x}}}{/tex}

2.821 = 2x

question_text: The following results have been obtained during the kinetic studies of the reaction. 2A + B {tex}\to{/tex} C + D Experiment [A]/mol L -1 [B]/mol L -1 Initial rate of formation of D/mol L -1 min -1 I 0.1 0.1 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 II 0.3 0.2 7.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 III 0.3 0.4 2.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -1 IV 0.4 0.1 2.40 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 Determine the rate law and the rate constant for the reaction.
question_html:
The following results have been obtained during the kinetic studies of the reaction.
2A + B {tex}\to{/tex} C + D

Experiment [A]/mol L-1 [B]/mol L-1 Initial rate of formation of D/mol L-1 min-1
I 0.1 0.1 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3
II 0.3 0.2 7.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2
III 0.3 0.4 2.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1
IV 0.4 0.1 2.40 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2

Determine the rate law and the rate constant for the reaction.
solution_text: From experiments I and IV, it may be noted that [B] is same but [A] has been made four times, the rate of reaction has also becomes four times. This means w.r.t A Rate {tex} \propto [A]{/tex} From experiments II and III it may be noted that [A] is kept same an [B] has been doubled, the rate of reaction has become four times. This means w.r.t B, Rate {tex} \propto {[B]^2}{/tex} Combining (i) and (ii), we get the rate law for the given reaction as: Rate {tex} = k\left[ A \right]{\left[ B \right]^2}{/tex} Thus order w.r.t A = 1 Order w.r.t B = 2 The overall order of the reaction = 1 + 2 = 3 The rate constant and its units can be calculated from the data of each experiment using the expression. {tex}k = \frac{{rate}}{{\left[ A \right]{{\left[ B \right]}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{{\min }^{ - 1}}}}{{\left( {mol\,{L^{ - 1}}} \right){{\left( {mol\,{L^{ - 1}}} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = mo{l^{ - 2}}{L^{ - 2}}{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex} Expt. {tex}k=mo{l^{ - 2}}{L^{ - 2}}{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{6.0 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.1 \times {{\left( {0.1} \right)}^2}}} = 6.0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{7.2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{0.3 \times {{\left( {0.2} \right)}^2}}} = 6.0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2.88 \times {{10}^{ - 1}}}}{{0.3 \times {{\left( {0.4} \right)}^2}}} = 6.0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2.4 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{0.4 \times {{\left( {0.1} \right)}^2}}} = 6.0{/tex} Rate constant {tex}k = 6.0\,mo{l^{ - 2}}{L^{ - 2}}{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
From experiments I and IV, it may be noted that [B] is same but [A] has been made four times, the rate of reaction has also becomes four times. This means w.r.t A
Rate {tex} \propto [A]{/tex}
From experiments II and III it may be noted that [A] is kept same an [B] has been doubled, the rate of reaction has become four times. This means w.r.t B,
Rate {tex} \propto {[B]^2}{/tex}
Combining (i) and (ii), we get the rate law for the given reaction as:
Rate {tex} = k\left[ A \right]{\left[ B \right]^2}{/tex}
Thus order w.r.t A = 1
Order w.r.t B = 2
The overall order of the reaction
= 1 + 2 = 3
The rate constant and its units can be calculated from the data of each experiment using the expression.
{tex}k = \frac{{rate}}{{\left[ A \right]{{\left[ B \right]}^2}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{{\min }^{ - 1}}}}{{\left( {mol\,{L^{ - 1}}} \right){{\left( {mol\,{L^{ - 1}}} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
{tex} = mo{l^{ - 2}}{L^{ - 2}}{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Expt. {tex}k=mo{l^{ - 2}}{L^{ - 2}}{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}

  1. {tex}\frac{{6.0 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.1 \times {{\left( {0.1} \right)}^2}}} = 6.0{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{{7.2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{0.3 \times {{\left( {0.2} \right)}^2}}} = 6.0{/tex}
  3. {tex}\frac{{2.88 \times {{10}^{ - 1}}}}{{0.3 \times {{\left( {0.4} \right)}^2}}} = 6.0{/tex}
  4. {tex}\frac{{2.4 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{0.4 \times {{\left( {0.1} \right)}^2}}} = 6.0{/tex}

Rate constant {tex}k = 6.0\,mo{l^{ - 2}}{L^{ - 2}}{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}

Therefore, the rate of the reaction is given by, {tex}Rate=k{{[A]}^{1}}{{[B]}^{0}}{/tex} Rate = k [A] From experiment I, we obtain {tex}2.0\times {{10}^{-2}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}=k(0.1\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}){/tex} {tex}k=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}{/tex} From experiment II, we obtain {tex}4.0\times {{10}^{-2}}\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}\,{{\min }^{-1}}=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}[A] {/tex} , so {tex}[A]=0.2mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{/tex} From experiment III, we obtain {tex}Rate=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}\times 0.4mol\,{{L}^{-1}} =0.08\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}{/tex} From experiment IV, we obtain {tex}2.0\times {{10}^{-2}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}=0.2\,{{\min }^{-1}}[A] {/tex} , so {tex}[A]=0.1mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{/tex}

The given reaction is of the first order with respect to A and of zero order with respect to B.
Therefore, the rate of the reaction is given by,
{tex}Rate=k{{[A]}^{1}}{{[B]}^{0}}{/tex}
Rate = k [A]
From experiment I, we obtain
{tex}2.0\times {{10}^{-2}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}=k(0.1\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}){/tex}
{tex}k=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}{/tex}
From experiment II, we obtain
{tex}4.0\times {{10}^{-2}}\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}\,{{\min }^{-1}}=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}[A] {/tex}, so {tex}[A]=0.2mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{/tex}
From experiment III, we obtain
{tex}Rate=0.2{{\min }^{-1}}\times 0.4mol\,{{L}^{-1}} =0.08\,mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}{/tex}
From experiment IV, we obtain
{tex}2.0\times {{10}^{-2}}mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{{\min }^{-1}}=0.2\,{{\min }^{-1}}[A] {/tex}, so {tex}[A]=0.1mol\,{{L}^{-1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Calculate the half-life of a first-order reaction from their rate constants given below: 200 S -1 2 min -1 4 years -1
question_html:
Calculate the half-life of a first-order reaction from their rate constants given below:

  1. 200 S-1
  2. 2 min-1
  3. 4 years-1
solution_text: The relationship between the half-life period of a first-order reaction is given by: {tex}t_{1/2} = \frac{{0.693}}{k}{/tex} {tex}t_{1/2} = \frac{{0.693}}{{200{s^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.346 \times {10^{ - 2}}s{/tex} {tex} = 3.46 \times {10^{ - 3}}s{/tex} {tex}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{2\,{{\min }^{ - 1}}}} {/tex} = 0.346 min {tex}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{4\;year{s^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 0.173 year
solution_html:
The relationship between the half-life period of a first-order reaction is given by:
{tex}t_{1/2} = \frac{{0.693}}{k}{/tex}

  1. {tex}t_{1/2} = \frac{{0.693}}{{200{s^{ - 1}}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 0.346 \times {10^{ - 2}}s{/tex}
    {tex} = 3.46 \times {10^{ - 3}}s{/tex}
  2. {tex}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{2\,{{\min }^{ - 1}}}} {/tex} = 0.346 min 
  3. {tex}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{4\;year{s^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 0.173 year

question_text: The half life for radioactive decay of 14 C is 5730 years. An archaeological artifact containing wood had only 80% of the 14 C found in a living tree. Estimate the age of the sample.
question_html: The half life for radioactive decay of 14C is 5730 years. An archaeological artifact containing wood had only 80% of the 14C found in a living tree. Estimate the age of the sample.
solution_text: Radioactive decay follows first order kinetics, Decay constant (K) {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{5730}}yea{r^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \,\frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{0.693/5730\,yea{r^{ - 1}}}}\log \,\frac{{100}}{{80}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303 \times 5730}}{{0.693}} \times 0.0969\,year{/tex} = 1845 years
solution_html:
Radioactive decay follows first order kinetics,
Decay constant (K)  {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{5730}}yea{r^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \,\frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{0.693/5730\,yea{r^{ - 1}}}}\log \,\frac{{100}}{{80}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303 \times 5730}}{{0.693}} \times 0.0969\,year{/tex}
= 1845 years

question_text: The rate constant for a first order reaction is 60 s -1 . How much time will it take to reduce the initial concentration of the reactant to its 1/16 th value?
question_html: The rate constant for a first order reaction is 60 s-1. How much time will it take to reduce the initial concentration of the reactant to its 1/16th value?
solution_text: It is known that, {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{60\,{s^{ - 1}}}}\log \frac{1}{{\frac{1}{{16}}}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{60\,{s^{ - 1}}}}\log 16{/tex} {tex} = 4.6 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,s{/tex} (approximately) Hence, the required time is {tex} 4.6 \times {10^{ - 2}}s.{/tex}
solution_html:
It is known that,
{tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{60\,{s^{ - 1}}}}\log \frac{1}{{\frac{1}{{16}}}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{60\,{s^{ - 1}}}}\log 16{/tex}
{tex} = 4.6 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,s{/tex} (approximately)
Hence, the required time is {tex} 4.6 \times {10^{ - 2}}s.{/tex}

question_text: During nuclear explosion, one of the products is 90 Sr with half-life of 28.1 years. If 1 {tex}\mu g{/tex} of 90 Sr was absorbed in the bones of a newly born baby instead of calcium, how much of it will remain after 10 years and 60 years if it is not lost metabolically.
question_html: During nuclear explosion, one of the products is 90Sr with half-life of 28.1 years. If 1 {tex}\mu g{/tex} of 90Sr was absorbed in the bones of a newly born baby instead of calcium, how much of it will remain after 10 years and 60 years if it is not lost metabolically.
solution_text: Here, {tex} k=\frac{0.693}{t{}^{1}/{}_{2}}=\frac{0.693}{28.1}{{y}^{-1}}{/tex} It is known that, {tex} t=\frac{2.303}{k}\log \frac{{{[R]}_{0}}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}10 = \frac{{2.303}}{{\frac{{0.693}}{{28.1}}}}\log \frac{1}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex}10 = \frac{{2.303}}{{\frac{{0.693}}{{28.1}}}}\left( { - \log [R]} \right){/tex} {tex}\log [R] = - \frac{{10 \times 0.693}}{{2.303 \times 28.1}}{/tex} [R] = antilog (- 0.1071) {tex}= anti\log \left( {\bar 1.8929} \right){/tex} {tex}=0.7814\mu g{/tex} Therefore, {tex} =0.7814\mu g{/tex} of {tex}^{90}Sr{/tex} will remain after 10 years. Again, {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k}\log \frac{{{[R]}_{0}}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}60 = \frac{{2.303}}{{\frac{{0.693}}{{28.1}}}}\log \frac{1}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex}\log [R] = \frac{{60 \times 0.693}}{{2.303 \times 28.1}}{/tex} [R] = antilog ( 0.6425) {tex} = anti\log \left( {\bar 1.3575} \right){/tex} {tex} = 0.2278\mu g{/tex} Therefore, {tex}0.2278\mu g,of ^{90}Sr{/tex} will remain after 60 years.
solution_html:
Here,{tex} k=\frac{0.693}{t{}^{1}/{}_{2}}=\frac{0.693}{28.1}{{y}^{-1}}{/tex}
It is known that,{tex} t=\frac{2.303}{k}\log \frac{{{[R]}_{0}}}{[R]}{/tex}
{tex}10 = \frac{{2.303}}{{\frac{{0.693}}{{28.1}}}}\log \frac{1}{{[R]}}{/tex}

question_text: For a first order reaction, show that time required for 99% completion is twice the time required for the completion of 90% of reaction.
question_html: For a first order reaction, show that time required for 99% completion is twice the time required for the completion of 90% of reaction.
solution_text: For a first order reaction, the time required for 99% completion is t 1 = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{100}{100-99}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log 100 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} For a first order reaction, the time required for 90% completion is t 2 = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}\log \frac{100}{100-90}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}\log 10{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} Therefore, t 1 = 2 t 2 Hence, the time required for 99% completion of a first order reaction is twice the time required for the completion of 90% of the reaction.
solution_html:
For a first order reaction, the time required for 99% completion is
t1 = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{100}{100-99}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log 100
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex}
For a first order reaction, the time required for 90% completion is
t2 = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}\log \frac{100}{100-90}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}\log 10{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex}
Therefore, t1 = 2 t2
Hence, the time required for 99% completion of a first order reaction is twice the time required for the completion of 90% of the reaction.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: For the decomposition of azoisopropane to hexane and nitrogen at 543 K, the following data are obtained. t(sec) P(mm of Hg) 0 35.0 360 54.0 720 63.0 Calculate the rate constant.
question_html:
For the decomposition of azoisopropane to hexane and nitrogen at 543 K, the following data are obtained.

t(sec) P(mm of Hg)
0 35.0
360 54.0
720 63.0

Calculate the rate constant.
solution_text: The decomposition of azoisopropane to hexane and nitrogen at 543 K is represented by the equation given below: {tex}\mathop {{{\left( {C{H_3}} \right)}_2}CHN = NCH{{\left( {C{H_3}} \right)}_{2(g)}}}\limits_{\mathop {{P_0}}\limits_{{P_0} - p} } \to \mathop {{N_{2(g)}}}\limits_{\mathop 0\limits_p } + \mathop {{C_6}{H_{14(g)}}}\limits_{\mathop 0\limits_p } {/tex} After time, t, total pressure, {tex} {P_t} = \left( {{P_0} - p} \right) + p + p{/tex} {tex}{P_t} = {P_0} + p{/tex} {tex}p = {P_t} - {P_0}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}{P_0} - p = {P_0} - \left( {{P_t} - {P_0}} \right) = 2{P_0} - {P_t}{/tex} For a first order reaction, {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{P_0}}}{{{P_0} - p}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{P_0}}}{{2{P_0} - {P_t}}}{/tex} When t = 360 s, {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{{360\,s}}\log \frac{{35.0}}{{(2 \times 35.0) - 54.0}}{/tex} {tex}= 2.175 \times {10^{ - 3}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} When t = 720 s, {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{{720\,s}}\log \frac{{35.0}}{{(2 \times 35.0) - 63.0}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.235 \times {10^{ - 3}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Hence, the average value of rate constant is {tex}k = \frac{{\left( {2.175 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right) + \left( {2.235 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)}}{2}{s^{ - 1}} = 2.21 \times {10^{ - 3}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The decomposition of azoisopropane to hexane and nitrogen at 543 K is represented by the equation given below: 
{tex}\mathop {{{\left( {C{H_3}} \right)}_2}CHN = NCH{{\left( {C{H_3}} \right)}_{2(g)}}}\limits_{\mathop {{P_0}}\limits_{{P_0} - p} } \to \mathop {{N_{2(g)}}}\limits_{\mathop 0\limits_p } + \mathop {{C_6}{H_{14(g)}}}\limits_{\mathop 0\limits_p } {/tex}
After time, t, total pressure,{tex} {P_t} = \left( {{P_0} - p} \right) + p + p{/tex}
{tex}{P_t} = {P_0} + p{/tex}
{tex}p = {P_t} - {P_0}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}{P_0} - p = {P_0} - \left( {{P_t} - {P_0}} \right) = 2{P_0} - {P_t}{/tex}
For a first order reaction,
{tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{P_0}}}{{{P_0} - p}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{P_0}}}{{2{P_0} - {P_t}}}{/tex}
When t = 360 s,{tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{{360\,s}}\log \frac{{35.0}}{{(2 \times 35.0) - 54.0}}{/tex}
{tex}= 2.175 \times {10^{ - 3}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
When t = 720 s, {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{{720\,s}}\log \frac{{35.0}}{{(2 \times 35.0) - 63.0}}{/tex}
{tex} = 2.235 \times {10^{ - 3}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Hence, the average value of rate constant is
{tex}k = \frac{{\left( {2.175 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right) + \left( {2.235 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)}}{2}{s^{ - 1}} = 2.21 \times {10^{ - 3}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The following data were obtained during the first order thermal decomposition of SO 2 Cl 2 at a constant volume. SO 2 Cl 2 (g) {tex}\to{/tex} SO 2 (g) + Cl 2 (g) Experiment Time/ s -1 Total Pressure/ atm 1 0 0.5 2 100 0.6 Calculate the rate of the reaction when total pressure is 0.65 atm.
question_html:
The following data were obtained during the first order thermal decomposition of SO2Cl2 at a constant volume.
SO2Cl2 (g) {tex}\to{/tex} SO2 (g) + Cl2 (g)

Experiment Time/ s-1 Total Pressure/ atm
1 0 0.5
2 100 0.6

Calculate the rate of the reaction when total pressure is 0.65 atm.
solution_text: {tex}S{O_2}C{l_2}(g) \to S{O_2}(g) + C{l_2}(g){/tex} {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{P_1}}}{{2{P_1} - {P_t}}}{/tex} Where {tex}{{P_1}}{/tex} is the initial pressure at time t = 0 {tex}{{P_t}}{/tex} = total pressure When t = 100 s, for first-order reaction we have {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{{100\,\,\operatorname{Sec} }}\log \frac{{0.5}}{{2 \times 0.5 - 0.6}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{100\,s}}\log \left( {1.25} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{100\,s}}\left( {0.0969} \right){/tex} {tex} = 2.2316 \times {10^{ - 3}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} When {tex}{P_t} = 0.65atm{/tex} {tex}{P_1} + P = 0.65{/tex} {tex}P = 0.65 - {P_1}{/tex} {tex}= 0.65 - 0.50 = 0.15\;atm{/tex} Pressure of {tex}S{O_2}C{l_2}{/tex} at time t {tex}\left( {{P_{S{O_2}C{l_2}}}} \right) = {P_1} - P{/tex} = 0.5 - 0.15 = 0.35 atm Rate at that time {tex} = k \times {P_{S{O_2}C{l_2}}}{/tex} {tex} = \left( {2.2316 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}{s^{ - 1}}} \right){/tex} (0.35 atm) {tex} = 7.8 \times {10^{ - 5}}atm\,{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}S{O_2}C{l_2}(g) \to S{O_2}(g) + C{l_2}(g){/tex}
{tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{P_1}}}{{2{P_1} - {P_t}}}{/tex}
Where {tex}{{P_1}}{/tex} is the initial pressure at time t = 0
{tex}{{P_t}}{/tex} = total pressure
When t = 100 s, for first-order reaction we have
{tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{{100\,\,\operatorname{Sec} }}\log \frac{{0.5}}{{2 \times 0.5 - 0.6}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{100\,s}}\log \left( {1.25} \right){/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{100\,s}}\left( {0.0969} \right){/tex}
{tex} = 2.2316 \times {10^{ - 3}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
When {tex}{P_t} = 0.65atm{/tex}
{tex}{P_1} + P = 0.65{/tex}
{tex}P = 0.65 - {P_1}{/tex}
{tex}= 0.65 - 0.50 = 0.15\;atm{/tex}
Pressure of {tex}S{O_2}C{l_2}{/tex} at time t
{tex}\left( {{P_{S{O_2}C{l_2}}}} \right) = {P_1} - P{/tex}
= 0.5 - 0.15
= 0.35 atm
Rate at that time {tex} = k \times {P_{S{O_2}C{l_2}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \left( {2.2316 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}{s^{ - 1}}} \right){/tex} (0.35 atm)
{tex} = 7.8 \times {10^{ - 5}}atm\,{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}

question_text: The rate constant for the decomposition of hydrocarbons is 2.418 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 s -1 at 546 K. If the energy of activation is 179.9 kJ/mol, what will be the value of pre-exponential factor.
question_html: The rate constant for the decomposition of hydrocarbons is 2.418 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 s-1 at 546 K. If the energy of activation is 179.9 kJ/mol, what will be the value of pre-exponential factor.
solution_text: {tex}k = 2.418 \times {10^{ - 5}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} T= 546 K {tex}{E_a} = 179.9kJmo{l^{ - 1}} = 179.9 \times {10^3}\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} According to the Arrhenius equation, {tex}k = A{e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex} {tex}{\rm{In k = In A - }}\frac{{{E_a}}}{{RT}}{/tex} {tex}\log k = \log \,A - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex} {tex}\log A = \log k + \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex} {tex}= \log \left( {2.418 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}{s^{ - 1}}} \right) + \frac{{179.9 \times {{10}^3}J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314\,J{k^{ - 1}}\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 546K}}{/tex} = (0.3835 - 5) + 17.2082 = 12.5917 Therefore, A = antilog (12.5917) = {tex} 3.9 \times {10^{12}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} (approximately)
solution_html:
{tex}k = 2.418 \times {10^{ - 5}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
T= 546 K
{tex}{E_a} = 179.9kJmo{l^{ - 1}} = 179.9 \times {10^3}\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}

According to the Arrhenius equation,
{tex}k = A{e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex}
{tex}{\rm{In k = In A - }}\frac{{{E_a}}}{{RT}}{/tex}
{tex}\log k = \log \,A - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex}
{tex}\log A = \log k + \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex}
{tex}= \log \left( {2.418 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}{s^{ - 1}}} \right) + \frac{{179.9 \times {{10}^3}J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314\,J{k^{ - 1}}\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 546K}}{/tex}
= (0.3835 - 5) + 17.2082
= 12.5917

Therefore, A = antilog (12.5917)
={tex} 3.9 \times {10^{12}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} (approximately)

question_text: Consider a certain reaction A {tex}\to{/tex} Products with k = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 s -1 . Calculate the concentration of A remaining after 100 s if the initial concentration of A is 1.0 mol L -1 .
question_html: Consider a certain reaction A {tex}\to{/tex} Products with k = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 s-1. Calculate the concentration of A remaining after 100 s if the initial concentration of A is 1.0 mol L-1.
solution_text: {tex}k = 2.0 \times {10^{ - 2}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} T= 100 s {tex}{[A]_0} = 1.0\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} Since the unit of k is {tex}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} , the given reaction is a first order reaction. Therefore, {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{[A]}_0}}}{{[A]}}{/tex} {tex}2.0 \times {10^{ - 2}}{s^{ - 1}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{100}}\log \frac{{1.0}}{{[A]}}{/tex} {tex}2.0 \times {10^{ - 2}}{s^{ - 1}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{100}}\left( { - \log [A]} \right){/tex} {tex} - \log [A] = \frac{{2.0 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times 100}}{{2.303}}{/tex} [A] = antilog {tex} \left( { - \frac{{2.0 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times 100}}{{2.303}}} \right){/tex} = 0.135 mol L -1 (approximately) Hence, the remaining concentration of A is 0.135 mol L -1 .
solution_html:
{tex}k = 2.0 \times {10^{ - 2}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
T= 100 s
{tex}{[A]_0} = 1.0\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Since the unit of k is {tex}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}, the given reaction is a first order reaction.
Therefore, {tex}k = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{[A]}_0}}}{{[A]}}{/tex}
{tex}2.0 \times {10^{ - 2}}{s^{ - 1}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{100}}\log \frac{{1.0}}{{[A]}}{/tex}
{tex}2.0 \times {10^{ - 2}}{s^{ - 1}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{100}}\left( { - \log [A]} \right){/tex}
{tex} - \log [A] = \frac{{2.0 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times 100}}{{2.303}}{/tex}
[A] = antilog {tex} \left( { - \frac{{2.0 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times 100}}{{2.303}}} \right){/tex}
= 0.135 mol L-1 (approximately)
Hence, the remaining concentration of A is 0.135 mol L-1.

question_text: Sucrose decomposes in acid solution into glucose and fructose according to the first order rate law with t 1/2 = 3.00 hours . What fraction of the sample of sucrose remains after 8 hours.
question_html: Sucrose decomposes in acid solution into glucose and fructose according to the first order rate law with t1/2 = 3.00 hours . What fraction of the sample of sucrose remains after 8 hours.
solution_text: As sucrose decomposes according to the first order rate law, {tex}K = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} The aim is to find {tex}[R]/{[R]_0}{/tex} {tex}t1/2 = 3.00\;hour{/tex} {tex}K = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{3\,hr}}{/tex} = 0.231 hr -1 Hence, {tex}0.231\,h{r^{ - 1}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{8hr}}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} or {tex}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}} = 0.8024{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} = Antilog (0.8024) = 6.345 {tex}\frac{{[R]}}{{[R]}} = \frac{1}{{6.345}}{/tex} = 0.158
solution_html:
As sucrose decomposes according to the first order rate law,
{tex}K = \frac{{2.303}}{t}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
The aim is to find {tex}[R]/{[R]_0}{/tex}
{tex}t1/2 = 3.00\;hour{/tex}
{tex}K = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{3\,hr}}{/tex}
= 0.231 hr-1
Hence,
{tex}0.231\,h{r^{ - 1}} = \frac{{2.303}}{{8hr}}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
or {tex}\log \frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}} = 0.8024{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex}= Antilog (0.8024)
= 6.345
{tex}\frac{{[R]}}{{[R]}} = \frac{1}{{6.345}}{/tex}= 0.158

question_text: The decomposition of hydrocarbon follows the equation k = (4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 11 s -1 ) e -28000k/t . Calculate E a .
question_html: The decomposition of hydrocarbon follows the equation k = (4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 s-1) e-28000k/t. Calculate Ea.
solution_text: The expression for k is as follows: {tex}k = \left( {4.5 \times {{10}^{11}}{s^{ - 1}}} \right){e^{ - 28000}}K/T{/tex} ..... (i) Arrhenius equation is given by, {tex}k = A{e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex} ..... (ii) From equation (i) and (ii), we obtain {tex}\frac{{{E_a}}}{{RT}} = \frac{{28000K}}{T}{/tex} {tex}{E_a} = R \times 28000\,K{/tex} {tex} = 8.314\,J{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 28000\,K{/tex} {tex} = 232792\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}} = 232.792\,KJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The expression for k is as follows:
 {tex}k = \left( {4.5 \times {{10}^{11}}{s^{ - 1}}} \right){e^{ - 28000}}K/T{/tex} ..... (i)
Arrhenius equation is given by,
{tex}k = A{e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex} ..... (ii)
From equation (i) and (ii), we obtain
{tex}\frac{{{E_a}}}{{RT}} = \frac{{28000K}}{T}{/tex}
{tex}{E_a} = R \times 28000\,K{/tex}
{tex} = 8.314\,J{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 28000\,K{/tex}

question_html:
The rate constant for the first order decomposition of H2O2 is given by the following equation:
log k = 14.34 - 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 K/T
Calculate Ea for this reaction and at what temperature will its half-period be 256 minutes?
solution_text: Arrhenius equation is given by, {tex}k = A{e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex} {tex}In\,k = In\,A - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{RT}}{/tex} {tex}\log k = \log A - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex} The given equation is {tex}\log k = 14.34 - 1.25 \times {10^4}\,K/T{/tex} ..... (ii) From equation (i) and (ii), we obtain {tex}\frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}} = \frac{{1.25 \times {{10}^4}K}}{T}{/tex} {tex}{E_a} = 1.25 \times {10^4}K \times 2.303 \times R = 1.25 \times {10^4}K \times 2.303 \times 8.314\,J{K^{ - 1}}mol^{-1}{/tex} = 239339.3 J,mol -1 (approximately) = 239.34 kJ mol -1 Also, when {tex}{{\text{t}}_{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = 256 minutes {tex}k = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{256}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.707 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\min ^{ - 1}} = 4.51 \times {10^{ - 5}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} It is also given that, {tex}\log k = 14.34 - 1.25 \times 104\,K/T{/tex} {tex}\log \left( {4.51 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}} \right) = 14.34 - \frac{{1.25 \times {{10}^4}K}}{T}{/tex} {tex}\log \left( {0.654 - 05} \right) = 14.34 - \frac{{1.25 \times {{10}^4}K}}{T}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{1.25 \times {{10}^4}K}}{T} = 18.686{/tex} {tex}T = \frac{{1.25 \times {{10}^4}K}}{{18.686}}{/tex} = 668.95 K = 669 K (approximately)
solution_html:
Arrhenius equation is given by,
{tex}k = A{e^{ - {E_a}/RT}}{/tex}
{tex}In\,k = In\,A - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{RT}}{/tex}
{tex}\log k = \log A - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex}
The given equation is
{tex}\log k = 14.34 - 1.25 \times {10^4}\,K/T{/tex}..... (ii)
From equation (i) and (ii), we obtain
{tex}\frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}} = \frac{{1.25 \times {{10}^4}K}}{T}{/tex}
{tex}{E_a} = 1.25 \times {10^4}K \times 2.303 \times R = 1.25 \times {10^4}K \times 2.303 \times 8.314\,J{K^{ - 1}}mol^{-1}{/tex}
 = 239339.3 J,mol-1 (approximately)
 = 239.34 kJ mol-1
Also, when {tex}{{\text{t}}_{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = 256 minutes
{tex}k = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{256}}{/tex}
{tex} = 2.707 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\min ^{ - 1}} = 4.51 \times {10^{ - 5}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
It is also given that, {tex}\log k = 14.34 - 1.25 \times 104\,K/T{/tex}
{tex}\log \left( {4.51 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}} \right) = 14.34 - \frac{{1.25 \times {{10}^4}K}}{T}{/tex}
{tex}\log \left( {0.654 - 05} \right) = 14.34 - \frac{{1.25 \times {{10}^4}K}}{T}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{1.25 \times {{10}^4}K}}{T} = 18.686{/tex}
{tex}T = \frac{{1.25 \times {{10}^4}K}}{{18.686}}{/tex}
= 668.95 K
= 669 K (approximately)

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The decomposition of A into product has value of k as 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 s -1 at 10 o C and energy of activation 60 kJ mol -1 . At what temperature would k be 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 s -1 ?
question_html: The decomposition of A into product has value of k as 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 s-1 at 10oC and energy of activation 60 kJ mol-1. At what temperature would k be 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 s-1?
solution_text: The decomposition of A into product has value of k = {tex}4.5 \times {10^3}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} at 10°C and energy of activation 60 kJ {tex}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} . At what temperature would k be {tex}1.5 \times {10^4}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} ? Give data: {tex}k_1 =4.5 \times {10^3}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} ; {tex}k_2 = 1.5 \times {10^4}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} ; E a =60 kJ {tex}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} ; T 1 = 10°C = 10 + 273 = 283K ; T 2 = ? We know that, {tex}\log \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_1}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 R}}\left[ {\frac{{{T_2} - {T_1}}}{{{T_1}{T_2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \frac{{1.5 \times {{10}^4}{s^{ - 1}}}}{{4.5 \times {{10}^3}{s^{ - 1}}}} = \frac{{60 \times 1000\left( {{T_2} - 283} \right)}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 283{T_2}}}{/tex} {tex}{T_2} = \frac{{16980000}}{{57167}}{/tex} T 2 = 297 K = 297-273= 24°C
solution_html:
The decomposition of A into product has value of k = {tex}4.5 \times {10^3}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} at 10°C and energy of activation 60 kJ {tex}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}. At what temperature would k be {tex}1.5 \times {10^4}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}?
Give data:
{tex}k_1 =4.5 \times {10^3}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}; {tex}k_2 = 1.5 \times {10^4}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}; Ea =60 kJ {tex}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} ; T1 = 10°C = 10 + 273 = 283K ; T2 = ?
We know that,
{tex}\log \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_1}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 R}}\left[ {\frac{{{T_2} - {T_1}}}{{{T_1}{T_2}}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \log \frac{{1.5 \times {{10}^4}{s^{ - 1}}}}{{4.5 \times {{10}^3}{s^{ - 1}}}} = \frac{{60 \times 1000\left( {{T_2} - 283} \right)}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 283{T_2}}}{/tex}
{tex}{T_2} = \frac{{16980000}}{{57167}}{/tex}
T2= 297 K = 297-273= 24°C

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The time required for 10% completion of a first order reaction at 298 K is equal to that required for its 25% completion at 308 K. If the value of A is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 10 s -1 . Calculate k at 318 K and E a .
question_html: The time required for 10% completion of a first order reaction at 298 K is equal to that required for its 25% completion at 308 K. If the value of A is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1010 s-1. Calculate k at 318 K and Ea.
solution_text: For a first order reaction, {tex} t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - x}}{/tex} At 298 K, {tex} t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{100}}{{90}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{0.1054}}{k}{/tex} At 308 K, {tex} t' = \frac{{2.303}}{{k'}}\log \frac{{100}}{{75}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.2877}}{{k'}}{/tex} According to the question, t = t' {tex}\frac{{0.1054}}{k} = \frac{{0.2877}}{{k'}}; \frac{{k'}}{k} = 2.7296{/tex} From Arrhenius equation, we obtain {tex}\log \frac{{k'}}{k} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303R}}\left( {\frac{{T' - T}}{{TT'}}} \right){/tex} {tex}\log \left( {2.7296} \right) = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left( {\frac{{308 - 298}}{{298 \times 308}}} \right){/tex} {tex}{E_a} = \frac{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times 308 \times \log \left( {2.7296} \right)}}{{308 - 298}} = 76640.096\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}= 76.64\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} To calculate k at 318 K, It is given that, {tex} A = 4 \times {10^{10}}{s^{ - 1}},T = 318\,K{/tex} Again, from Arrhenius equation, we obtain {tex}\log k = \log A - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex} {tex} = \log \left( {4 \times {{10}^{10}}} \right) - \frac{{76.64 \times {{10}^3}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 318}}{/tex} = (0.6021+10) - 12.5876 = -1.9855 Therefore, k = Antilog (-1.9855) = {tex} 1.034 \times {10^{ - 2}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
For a first order reaction, {tex} t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - x}}{/tex}
At 298 K, {tex} t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{100}}{{90}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{{0.1054}}{k}{/tex}
At 308 K, {tex} t' = \frac{{2.303}}{{k'}}\log \frac{{100}}{{75}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.2877}}{{k'}}{/tex}

According to the question, t = t'
{tex}\frac{{0.1054}}{k} = \frac{{0.2877}}{{k'}}; \frac{{k'}}{k} = 2.7296{/tex}
From Arrhenius equation, we obtain
{tex}\log \frac{{k'}}{k} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303R}}\left( {\frac{{T' - T}}{{TT'}}} \right){/tex}
{tex}\log \left( {2.7296} \right) = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left( {\frac{{308 - 298}}{{298 \times 308}}} \right){/tex}
{tex}{E_a} = \frac{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times 308 \times \log \left( {2.7296} \right)}}{{308 - 298}} = 76640.096\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}= 76.64\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}

To calculate k at 318 K,
It is given that,{tex} A = 4 \times {10^{10}}{s^{ - 1}},T = 318\,K{/tex}

Again, from Arrhenius equation, we obtain
{tex}\log k = \log A - \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303\,RT}}{/tex}
{tex} = \log \left( {4 \times {{10}^{10}}} \right) - \frac{{76.64 \times {{10}^3}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 318}}{/tex}
= (0.6021+10) - 12.5876
= -1.9855
Therefore, k = Antilog (-1.9855)
={tex} 1.034 \times {10^{ - 2}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The rate of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 293 K to 313 K. Calculate the energy of activation of the reaction assuming that it does not change with temperature.
question_html: The rate of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 293 K to 313 K. Calculate the energy of activation of the reaction assuming that it does not change with temperature.
solution_text: From Arrhenius equation, we obtain {tex} \log \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_1}}} = \frac{{{{\rm E}_a}}}{{2.303R}}\left( {\frac{{{T_2} - {T_1}}}{{{T_1}{T_2}}}} \right){/tex} It is given that, {tex}{k_2} = 4{k_1}{/tex} {tex}{T_1} = 293K; {T_2} = 313K{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\log \frac{{4{k_1}}}{{{k_2}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left( {\frac{{313 - 293}}{{293 \times 313}}} \right){/tex} {tex}0.6021 = \frac{{20 \times {E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 313}}{/tex} {tex}{E_a} = \frac{{0.6021 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 313}}{{20}}{/tex} {tex} = 52863.33\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}} = 52.86\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Hence, the required energy of activation is {tex}52.86\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}.{/tex}
solution_html:
From Arrhenius equation, we obtain {tex} \log \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_1}}} = \frac{{{{\rm E}_a}}}{{2.303R}}\left( {\frac{{{T_2} - {T_1}}}{{{T_1}{T_2}}}} \right){/tex}
It is given that, {tex}{k_2} = 4{k_1}{/tex}
{tex}{T_1} = 293K; {T_2} = 313K{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\log \frac{{4{k_1}}}{{{k_2}}} = \frac{{{E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314}}\left( {\frac{{313 - 293}}{{293 \times 313}}} \right){/tex}
{tex}0.6021 = \frac{{20 \times {E_a}}}{{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 313}}{/tex}
{tex}{E_a} = \frac{{0.6021 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 293 \times 313}}{{20}}{/tex}
{tex} = 52863.33\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}} = 52.86\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Hence, the required energy of activation is {tex}52.86\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}}.{/tex}
